input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **system_under_study** in the provided documents primarily focuses on **cyber-biophysical systems** (Document [7]) and **automotive systems** (Documents [8], [9], [12], [14], [17]), with additional references to **manufacturing systems** (Document [10]), **aerospace applications** (Document [16]), and **battery systems** (Document [17]). Key characteristics include:

1. **Cyber-Biophysical Systems** (Document [7]):
   - A **hybrid system** integrating **physical, biological, and cyber components** (e.g., IoT sensors, actuators, and biological processes like cell cultures or microbial systems).
   - **Operational domain**: Real-time monitoring, control, and simulation of **interconnected cyber-physical-biological (CPB) processes**, often in **research or industrial biotechnology** (e.g., bioreactors, microbial fuel cells).
   - **Main components**:
     - **Physical layer**: Biological entities (e.g., cells, enzymes) or engineered materials interacting with environmental conditions.
     - **Cyber layer**: Embedded sensors (e.g., pH, temperature, oxygen sensors), actuators, and communication protocols (e.g., **MQTT, OPC UA**) for data acquisition.
     - **Biophysical layer**: Models capturing **biochemical reactions, growth dynamics, or material degradation** (e.g., using **ordinary differential equations (ODEs) or partial differential equations (PDEs)**).
   - **Key characteristics**:
     - **Dynamic and adaptive**: Systems exhibit **time-varying behaviors** influenced by biological feedback loops.
     - **High fidelity requirements**: Demand **real-time synchronization** between physical and digital representations to ensure accuracy in **predictive modeling** and **control**.
     - **Multi-scale modeling**: Integration of **macro-scale (e.g., reactor-level) and micro-scale (e.g., cellular-level) dynamics**.

2. **Automotive Systems** (Documents [8], [9], [12], [14]):
   - **Type**: **Vehicle systems** (e.g., **electric vehicles (EVs), autonomous cars, or connected vehicles**) and **manufacturing processes** (e.g., **assembly lines, supply chains**).
   - **Main components**:
     - **Physical layer**: Vehicles (e.g., **battery packs, powertrain, chassis**) or manufacturing equipment (e.g., **robotic arms, CNC machines**).
     - **Cyber layer**: **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication**, **embedded ECUs (Electronic Control Units)**, and **cloud-edge architectures** for data processing.
     - **Digital layer**: **High-fidelity simulations** (e.g., **MATLAB/Simulink, ANSYS Fluent**) for **aerodynamics, thermal management, or battery degradation**.
   - **Operational domain**:
     - **Automotive manufacturing**: Digital twins enable **real-time monitoring of production lines**, **predictive maintenance**, and **quality control** (e.g., ISO 23247 compliance).
     - **Vehicle operations**: Used for **fleet management**, **autonomous driving simulations**, and **battery health monitoring** (Document [17]).
   - **Key characteristics**:
     - **Real-time data fusion**: Integration of **sensor data (e.g., LiDAR, IMU), telematics, and simulation models** via **edge computing** (Document [14]).
     - **Standardized frameworks**: Adherence to **ISO 23247** for **manufacturing digital twins** and **ISO/IEC 30173** for **conceptual definitions**.
     - **Multi-domain simulation**: Combines **mechanical, electrical, thermal, and control systems** (e.g., **Modelica-based co-simulation**).

3. **Manufacturing Systems** (Document [10]):
   - **Type**: **Industrial manufacturing processes** (e.g., **discrete manufacturing, process industries**).
   - **Main components**:
     - **Physical layer**: Machines (e.g., **lathes, 3D printers**), assembly lines, and supply chain nodes.
     - **Cyber layer**: **Industrial IoT (IIoT) devices**, **PLCs (Programmable Logic Controllers)**, and **cloud platforms** (e.g., **Siemens MindSphere, PTC ThingWorx**).
   - **Operational domain**: **Digital twin frameworks** (ISO 23247-1) for **process optimization, energy efficiency, and defect prediction**.
   - **Key characteristics**:
     - **Holistic modeling**: Integrates **CAD/CAM data, real-time sensor streams, and digital thread** for **end-to-end process tracking**.
     - **Standardized architecture**: Follows **ISO 23247** for **digital twin maturity levels** (e.g., **Level 1: Static Model → Level 4: Closed-Loop Control**).

4. **Aerospace Systems** (Document [16]):
   - **Type**: **Satellite systems** (e.g., **on-orbit collision avoidance, space debris detection**).
   - **Main components**:
     - **Physical layer**: Satellites, sensors (e.g., **radar, optical telescopes**), and orbital mechanics models.
     - **Cyber layer**: **Ground stations, cloud-based processing**, and **AI-driven anomaly detection**.
   - **Operational domain**: **Real-time orbital dynamics simulation** and **traffic management**.
   - **Key characteristics**:
     - **Adaptation of ISO 23247**: Tailored for **space-based applications**, focusing on **high-precision trajectory modeling** and **collision risk assessment**.
     - **Dynamic environment**: Accounts for **variable gravitational forces, solar radiation pressure, and debris clouds**.

5. **Battery Systems** (Document [17]):
   - **Type**: **Lithium-ion battery packs** (e.g., **EV batteries, energy storage systems**).
   - **Main components**:
     - **Physical layer**: Battery cells, cooling systems, and electrical circuits.
     - **Cyber layer**: **State-of-charge (SoC) sensors, temperature sensors, and BMS (Battery Management Systems)**.
   - **Operational domain**: **Lifetime prediction, thermal management, and degradation modeling**.
   - **Key characteristics**:
     - **Multi-physics simulation**: Combines **electrochemical models (e.g., **P2D models**), thermal models, and **mechanical stress analysis**.
     - **ISO 23247 adoption**: Framework applied for **battery digital twins** to enable **predictive maintenance and cycle life estimation**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT) services** provided in the documents are categorized by **service type**, **target users**, and **capabilities**, with a focus on **automotive, manufacturing, cyber-biophysical, aerospace, and battery systems**. Key services include:

1. **Monitoring Services** (Documents [7], [8], [9], [10], [12], [14], [16], [17]):
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **Real-time telemetry**: Continuous streaming of **sensor data** (e.g., **vibration, temperature, pressure**) from physical systems to the DT via **OPC UA, MQTT, or REST APIs**.
     - **Anomaly detection**: Use of **machine learning (ML) models** (e.g., **isolated forest, LSTM networks**) to identify **deviations from nominal operation** (e.g., **battery degradation, equipment wear**).
     - **Health monitoring**: **Condition-based maintenance (CBM)** for **predictive failure analysis** (e.g., **RUL - Remaining Useful Life estimation** for batteries or machinery).
   - **Target users**:
     - **Maintenance engineers** (for **predictive maintenance**).
     - **Operational managers** (for **real-time dashboards**).
     - **Researchers** (for **experimental validation** of cyber-biophysical models).

2. **Optimization Services** (Documents [8], [9], [10], [14], [15]):
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **Process optimization**: **Multi-objective optimization** (e.g., **minimizing energy consumption, maximizing throughput**) using **genetic algorithms or reinforcement learning**.
     - **Supply chain coordination**: **Digital twin-enabled supply chain networks** optimize **inventory levels, logistics routes, and production scheduling** (e.g., **automotive manufacturing**).
     - **Thermal/energy management**: **Dynamic thermal control** for **batteries or electronic components** via **model predictive control (MPC)**.
   - **Target users**:
     - **Production planners** (for **resource allocation**).
     - **Energy managers** (for **grid integration and demand response**).
     - **Automotive OEMs** (for **vehicle performance tuning**).

3. **Prediction Services** (Documents [7], [9], [12], [14], [15], [16]):
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **Failure prediction**: **Time-series forecasting** (e.g., **ARIMA, Prophet**) for **equipment failures** or **battery degradation**.
     - **Performance forecasting**: **Simulation-driven predictions** of **vehicle range (EVs), aerodynamic drag, or manufacturing yield**.
     - **Trajectory prediction**: **Orbital mechanics simulation** for **satellite collision avoidance** (Document [16]) using **high-fidelity orbital dynamics models**.
     - **Biophysical predictions**: **Growth rate modeling** for **microbial cultures** or **material degradation** in cyber-biophysical systems (Document [7]).
   - **Target users**:
     - **Data scientists** (for **model training and validation**).
     - **Policymakers** (for **regulatory compliance and safety**).
     - **Autonomous vehicle developers** (for **road condition prediction**).

4. **Control Services** (Documents [7], [10], [14], [16]):
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **Closed-loop control**: **Real-time feedback control** (e.g., **PID, MPC**) for **autonomous systems, industrial robots, or battery charging**.
     - **Adaptive control**: **Reinforcement learning-based controllers** for **dynamic environments** (e.g., **aerospace collision avoidance**).
     - **Digital twin-in-the-loop (DTIL)**: **Co-simulation of physical and digital systems** for **control strategy validation** (e.g., **autonomous driving scenarios**).
   - **Target users**:
     - **Control engineers** (for **system stability analysis**).
     - **Robotics teams** (for **motion planning**).
     - **Aerospace mission planners** (for **orbital maneuvering**).

5. **Visualization Services** (Documents [8], [9], [12], [15], [17]):
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **3D/4D visualization**: **Interactive digital replicas** (e.g., **Unity, Unreal Engine, or custom web-based dashboards**) for **spatial analysis** (e.g., **vehicle interior, factory layout**).
     - **Data-driven dashboards**: **Real-time KPI visualization** (e.g., **energy consumption, defect rates**) using **Power BI, Tableau, or custom DT platforms**.
     - **Augmented Reality (AR)/Virtual Reality (VR)**: **Immersive training simulations** for **automotive technicians or aerospace operators**.
     - **Multi-scale visualization**: **Zooming from macro (e.g., factory) to micro (e.g., cellular-level)** in cyber-biophysical systems (Document [7]).
   - **Target users**:
     - **Technicians** (for **troubleshooting and training**).
     - **Executives** (for **strategic decision-making**).
     - **Researchers** (for **experimental hypothesis testing**).

6. **Simulation Services** (Documents [10], [12], [14], [15], [16]):
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **High-fidelity simulation**: **Co-simulation of multi-physics domains** (e.g., **MATLAB/Simulink, ANSYS, COMSOL**) for **aerodynamics, thermal, or structural analysis**.
     - **AI-driven simulation**: **Neural network surrogates** (e.g., **physics-informed neural networks**) for **fast predictive modeling**.
     - **Digital twin-as-a-service (DTaaS)**: **Cloud-based simulation platforms** (e.g., **AWS IoT TwinMaker, Microsoft Azure Digital Twins**) for **remote access**.
   - **Target users**:
     - **Engineers** (for **design validation**).
     - **Academics** (for **scientific research**).
     - **Startups** (for **prototyping without physical hardware**).

7. **Interoperability Services** (Documents [10], [12], [14], [16]):
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **Standardized data exchange**: **OPC UA, ROS (Robot Operating System), or DDS (Data Distribution Service)** for **cross-platform communication**.
     - **Semantic interoperability**: **Ontology-based data integration** (e.g., **OWL, RDF**) for **knowledge sharing** (e.g., **ISO/IEC 30173 terminology**).
     - **Edge-cloud synergy**: **Distributed DT architectures** where **edge nodes process local data** and **cloud hosts global models** (Document [14]).
   - **Target users**:
     - **IT architects** (for **system integration**).
     - **Data engineers** (for **ETL pipelines**).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **tooling and enablers** for Digital Twin systems in the provided documents include **software platforms, development tools, simulation engines, databases, and specialized technologies**. Below is a structured breakdown by **category** and **role**:

---

### **1. Digital Twin Frameworks and Standards**
- **ISO 23247-1:2021** (Document [10]):
  - **Role**: Provides a **standardized framework** for **manufacturing digital twins**, defining **maturity levels (1–4)** and **architectural principles** (e.g., **data synchronization, simulation, and control loops**).
  - **Key components**:
    - **Digital Twin Maturity Model (DTTMM)**: Classifies DTs from **static models (Level 1)** to **closed-loop control (Level 4)**.
    - **Digital Thread**: Enables **end-to-end data flow** from **design to operation**.

- **ISO/IEC 30173:2023** (Document [11]):
  - **Role**: Defines **concepts and terminology** for digital twins, ensuring **interoperability** across domains (e.g., **automotive, aerospace, cyber-biophysical**).
  - **Key terms standardized**:
    - **Digital Twin System (DTS)**: The **entire ecosystem** (physical + digital + services).
    - **Digital Twin Model (DTM)**: The **computational representation** of the physical system.

- **Adapted ISO 23247 for Aerospace** (Document [16]):
  - **Role**: Tailors **ISO 23247** for **satellite systems**, focusing on **orbital dynamics simulation** and **collision avoidance algorithms**.
  - **Enablers**:
    - **High-precision ephemeris models** (e.g., **SGP4, JPL DE405**).
    - **AI-driven debris tracking** (e.g., **YOLACT for object detection**).

---

### **2. Simulation Engines and Modeling Tools**
- **MATLAB/Simulink** (Documents [8], [9], [14], [15]):
  - **Role**: **Multi-domain simulation** for **control systems, aerodynamics, and thermal management**.
  - **Use cases**:
    - **Vehicle dynamics** (e.g., **EV powertrain modeling**).
    - **Cyber-biophysical systems** (e.g., **bioreactor control**).
  - **Integration**: Supports **co-simulation with ANSYS, COMSOL, and ROS**.

- **ANSYS Fluent** (Documents [8], [9]):
  - **Role**: **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** for **aerodynamics, thermal analysis, and battery cooling**.
  - **Use cases**:
    - **Automotive aerodynamics** (e.g., **drag reduction**).
    - **Battery thermal management** (Document [17]).

- **COMSOL Multiphysics** (Document [15]):
  - **Role**: **Multi-physics simulation** (e.g., **electrochemical, structural, thermal**) for **battery systems and industrial processes**.
  - **Use cases**:
    - **Lithium-ion battery degradation modeling** (Document [17]).
    - **Material fatigue analysis** in manufacturing.

- **Modelica** (Document [15]):
  - **Role**: **Functional mock-up interface (FMI)** for **co-simulation** between **different engineering tools** (e.g., **Simulink + COMSOL**).
  - **Use cases**:
    - **Automotive powertrain co-simulation**.
    - **Cyber-biophysical hybrid models**.

- **ROS (Robot Operating System)** (Documents [12], [14]):
  - **Role**: **Real-time data processing and control** for **autonomous systems and industrial robots**.
  - **Integration**:
    - **OPC UA bridges** for **IIoT interoperability**.
    - **Edge computing** for **low-latency control** (Document [14]).

---

### **3. Cloud and Edge Computing Platforms**
- **AWS IoT TwinMaker** (Document [15]):
  - **Role**: **Cloud-based DT platform** for **3D visualization, simulation, and AI integration**.
  - **Features**:
    - **Pre-built connectors** for **OPC UA, MQTT, and REST APIs**.
    - **AI/ML integration** (e.g., **SageMaker for anomaly detection**).

- **Microsoft Azure Digital Twins** (Document [15]):
  - **Role**: **Enterprise-grade DT platform** with **graph-based modeling** and **real-time analytics**.
  - **Use cases**:
    - **Automotive supply chain optimization**.
    - **Industrial asset monitoring**.

- **Edge Computing Frameworks** (Document [14]):
  - **Role**: **Distributed DT processing** to reduce **cloud latency**.
  - **Technologies**:
    - **Kubernetes-based edge clusters** for **local data processing**.
    - **Federated learning** for **privacy-preserving AI training** on edge devices.
  - **Use cases**:
    - **Automotive V2X communication** (e.g., **real-time collision avoidance**).
    - **Manufacturing predictive maintenance**.

---

### **4. Data Management and Databases**
- **OPC UA** (Documents [7], [8], [12], [14], [16]):
  - **Role**: **Industrial communication standard** for **real-time data exchange** between **PLCs, sensors, and DTs**.
  - **Use cases**:
    - **Cyber-biophysical sensor networks**.
    - **Automotive V2X and manufacturing IIoT**.

- **PostgreSQL + TimescaleDB** (Document [7]):
  - **Role**: **Time-series database** for **cyber-biophysical data** (e.g., **bioreactor conditions, microbial growth**).
  - **Features**:
    - **High-resolution time-stamped data**.
    - **SQL-based analytics** for **predictive modeling**.

- **Graph Databases (Neo4j)** (Document [15]):
  - **Role**: **Semantic data modeling** for **relationship-heavy DTs** (e.g., **supply chain networks, aerospace orbital mechanics**).
  - **Use cases**:
    - **Dependency mapping** in **automotive manufacturing**.
    - **Debris tracking networks** in aerospace (Document [16]).

---

### **5. AI/ML and Data Analytics Tools**
- **Python + Scikit-learn/PyTorch** (Documents [7], [15]):
  - **Role**: **ML model training** for **anomaly detection, prediction, and control**.
  - **Use cases**:
    - **Cyber-biophysical system monitoring** (e.g., **LSTM for microbial growth forecasting**).
    - **Automotive failure prediction** (e.g., **XGBoost for RUL estimation**).

- **TensorFlow/PyTorch + Digital Twin Integration** (Document [15]):
  - **Role**: **Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs)** for **fast surrogate modeling**.
  - **Use cases**:
    - **Battery degradation prediction** (Document [17]).
    - **Aerospace collision avoidance** (Document [16]).

- **Spark MLlib** (Document [14]):
  - **Role**: **Large-scale DT data processing** for **edge-cloud synergy**.
  - **Use cases**:
    - **Manufacturing defect detection**.
    - **Automotive fleet analytics**.

---

### **6. Development and Integration Tools**
- **GitLab/GitHub + CI/CD** (Document [15]):
  - **Role**: **Version control and automated testing** for **DT model development**.
  - **Use cases**:
    - **Collaborative DT platform development**.
    - **Model validation pipelines**.

- **Docker/Kubernetes** (Document [14]):
  - **Role**: **Containerization** for **scalable DT deployment** across **edge and cloud**.
  - **Use cases**:
    - **Automotive V2X microservices**.
    - **Industrial DT microservices**.

- **Semantic Web Technologies (OWL, RDF)** (Document [12]):
  - **Role**: **Ontology-based knowledge representation** for **DT interoperability**.
  - **Use cases**:
    - **Automotive system integration** (e.g., **ISO/IEC 30173 compliance**).
    - **Cyber-biophysical system modeling**.

---

### **7. Specialized Enablers**
- **Digital Thread Platforms** (Document [10]):
  - **Role**: **End-to-end data connectivity** from **design to operation** in **manufacturing DTs**.
  - **Tools**:
    - **Siemens Teamcenter, PTC Windchill**.
    - **Custom digital thread middleware** (e.g., **based on OPC UA**).

- **AR/VR Development Tools** (Document [8]):
  - **Role**: **Immersive DT visualization** for **training and maintenance**.
  - **Tools**:
    - **Unity, Unreal Engine, Microsoft HoloLens**.
    - **Custom web-based 3D viewers** (e.g., **Three.js, CesiumJS**).

- **Blockchain for DT Security** (Document [12]):
  - **Role**: **Tamper-proof data logging** for **trust in DT systems**.
  - **Use cases**:
    - **Automotive supply chain authenticity**.
    - **Cyber-biophysical experiment reproducibility**.

---
**Not in Document**:
- No information found on **specific blockchain implementations**, **quantum computing integration**, or **5G/6G-specific DT enablers** in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces a **100 ms clock synchronization accuracy** between physical and digital components to enable computation offloading and coordination between devices. This temporal constraint is mandated due to hardware limitations and aligns with stricter vehicular application standards. The system does not explicitly detail service-specific time scales beyond this synchronization requirement, though ROS2-based node execution and inter-node communication (via ROS2 topics/services) inherently introduce latencies dependent on network topology and hardware performance. No explicit temporal granularity needs for individual services (e.g., sensor fusion, actuation) are specified beyond the 100 ms synchronization constraint.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system follows a **decentralized, entity-based architecture** with three primary software components mapped to distinct hardware components: the *Physical Twin Entity*, *Digital Twin Entity*, and *User Entity*. Each entity is instantiated as an independent **ROS2 package**, containing one or more ROS2 nodes. This design deviates from ISO 23247 by consolidating resource-specific and general automotive functionalities (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*) within the *Physical Twin Entity*, rather than distributing them across multiple Function Entities (FEs). The system does not explicitly describe **multiple Digital Twin instances** or hierarchical coordination between them, nor does it detail centralized vs. decentralized trade-offs beyond the entity-based decomposition. Responsibilities are scoped to each entity’s domain (e.g., data pre-processing in *Physical Twin Entity*, visualization/control in *User Entity*), but no explicit multiplicities (e.g., twin replication, federated twins) are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system adopts an **ISO 23247 Reference Architecture (RA)-inspired orchestration** with **entity-based decomposition** and **ROS2-based component integration**. Key coordination mechanisms include:
   - **Functional Entities (FEs) mapped to ROS2 packages**, where each package encapsulates one or more ROS2 nodes implementing FE functionality.
   - **Inter-entity communication** via ROS2 topics/services, enabling data exchange between *Physical Twin Entity* (sensors/actuators), *Digital Twin Entity* (data management/control), and *User Entity* (3D visualization/control panel).
   - **Resource management** is implicitly handled by hardware-specific deployment of entities (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators), with the *Physical Twin Entity* acting as a generalized resource hub.
   - **Deviations from ISO 23247** include merging resource-specific and general automotive functionalities into the *Physical Twin Entity* and splitting the *User Interface FE* into *3D Visualization* and *Control Panel* subcomponents. No explicit mention of **orchestration frameworks** (e.g., Kubernetes, edge orchestration) or **system-wide coordination protocols** beyond ROS2 is provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system adheres to the **ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing**, specifically Part 2 (Reference Architecture), which defines an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)**. The system comprises **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**—physical entities such as equipment, facilities, products, personnel, or processes—that are digitally represented by **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)**. Each DTE is implemented via **Functional Entities (FEs)**, which may leverage **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** for standardized data exchange and interoperability. The system integrates **geometric, behavioral, and analytical models** to simulate OMEs, with **data sources** including real-time sensor feeds, historical operational data, and process logs. Data management follows an **entity-centric approach**, where each OME is associated with a **Data Collection and Device Control Entity (DCE)** responsible for bidirectional data flow (sensing and actuation). Model relationships are established through **functional decomposition** into FEs, enabling modularity and specialization (e.g., data transformation, human-machine interfaces). The **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)** serves as the core computational model, while **User Entities (UEs)** provide interfaces for monitoring and control. No explicit details on sampling frequencies, transmission rates, or model fidelity metrics (e.g., accuracy thresholds) are provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The ISO 23247 standard implicitly outlines **fidelity and validity considerations** through its **entity-based reference architecture**, which mandates **validation via observable physical presence and operation** of OMEs. While not explicitly detailed, the standard implies that **uncertainty quantification** must account for discrepancies between digital representations (DTEs) and physical OMEs. **Verification mechanisms** are inferred to rely on **real-time data synchronization** between DCEs and DTEs, ensuring consistency. However, specific **error detection/correction methods**, **quality assurance processes**, or **formal validation protocols** (e.g., statistical testing, uncertainty propagation frameworks) are not described in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves real-time data acquisition from physical sensors and their transformation into digital representations. Key data flows include:
- **Data Types Transmitted**:
  - Raw **LiDAR point cloud data** (2D plane representation of individual points).
  - **Vehicle state information** (e.g., motor actuation, steering servo status).
  - **Object tracks** (processed from raw LiDAR data via clustering and tracking).
  - **Projected driving path** (derived from vehicle state and sensor inputs).

- **Preprocessing Steps**:
  - Raw LiDAR data undergoes **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** preprocessing (implemented as a Software Function Entity, FE) to convert unstructured point clouds into structured object tracks and environmental representations.
  - **Data Pre-Processing FE** aligns sensor data streams (e.g., LiDAR, motor, steering servo) into standardized formats for downstream processing.

- **Communication Protocols**:
  - **ROS2** (Robot Operating System 2) for intra-system communication between Software Function Entities (FEs).
  - **Wi-Fi (UDP)** for inter-entity communication between Physical Twin Entities (e.g., sensors) and Digital Twin Entities.

- **Transmission Frequencies**:
  - Not explicitly quantified in the document, but implied to be **high-frequency** (typical for autonomous vehicle sensor streams, e.g., 10–100Hz for LiDAR and vehicle state updates).

- **Data Quality Assurance**:
  - **Data Translation FE** serializes data, aligns message types, and performs **unit conversions** to ensure consistency across heterogeneous data streams.
  - **Visualization Pre-processing** (Presentation FE) filters and validates data for real-time 3D visualization (e.g., object tracks, driving path).

- **Data Integration Mechanisms**:
  - **Data Collecting FE** aggregates raw sensor inputs (e.g., LiDAR, motor, steering servo) into a unified digital representation.
  - **Report Generation FE** stores processed data for post-monitoring (e.g., trajectory analysis, fault detection).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in this DTS is facilitated by the **ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control) Controlling FE**, which translates digital commands into physical actuation. Key components include:
- **Command Types**:
  - **Control signals** for **motor actuation** and **steering servo actuation**, derived from processed digital twin data (e.g., object tracks, driving path projections).
  - **ACC inputs** (e.g., desired speed, collision avoidance thresholds) translated via **Data Translation FE** to ensure compatibility with physical actuators.

- **Control Message Formats**:
  - Serialized and standardized via **Data Translation FE** to align with physical actuator interfaces (e.g., voltage/current commands for motors/servos).
  - **ROS2** is used for intra-system communication, while **Wi-Fi (UDP)** handles inter-entity command transmission to physical actuators.

- **Validation and Authentication Mechanisms**:
  - **Data Translation FE** validates command formats and performs **unit conversions** (e.g., mm/s to RPM for motors).
  - No explicit mention of cryptographic authentication, but **UDP reliability** is implied to rely on application-layer checks (e.g., checksums in control messages).

- **Feedback Systems**:
  - **Vehicle State FE** continuously monitors physical responses (e.g., motor RPM, steering angle) and feeds back into the digital twin for closed-loop control.
  - **3D Visualization FE** displays real-time adjustments (e.g., path deviations, object proximity) to validate control actions.

- **Conflict Resolution**:
  - Not explicitly detailed, but assumed to rely on **priority-based routing** in ROS2/UDP networks (e.g., emergency braking commands overriding speed adjustments).
- **Command Execution Confirmation**:
  - **Actuation FE** logs execution status (e.g., servo position, motor torque) and integrates feedback into the digital twin for adaptive control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin Technical Connection** infrastructure is designed for real-time, low-latency communication between entities. Key specifications include:
- **Communication Protocols**:
  - **ROS2** (Robot Operating System 2) for intra-system communication between Software Function Entities (FEs), enabling publish-subscribe messaging for sensor data, commands, and visualizations.
  - **Wi-Fi (UDP)** for inter-entity communication between Physical Twin Entities (sensors/actuators) and Digital Twin Entities, prioritizing **low-latency** over reliability (UDP’s stateless nature).

- **Network Topology and Architecture**:
  - **Distributed architecture** with clear separation between:
    - **Physical Twin Entity** (sensors/actuators).
    - **Digital Twin Entity** (processing, visualization, control FEs).
    - **User Entity** (3D visualization, control panel interfaces).
  - **Hierarchical data flow**: Sensors → Data Pre-Processing FE → Data Translation FE → Control/Visualization FEs → Actuators.

- **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
  - **Latency**: Implied to be **<100ms** (critical for autonomous driving applications; ROS2/UDP are optimized for this).
  - **Bandwidth**: Not explicitly quantified, but LiDAR data streams (e.g., 10–100Hz at 1M+ points) require **high-throughput Wi-Fi** (e.g., 802.11ac/n for sensor data).

- **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
  - **No explicit redundancy** mentioned, but ROS2 supports **node recovery** and **message queuing** for transient failures.
  - **Data Translation FE** ensures **serialization consistency**, reducing corruption risks during transmission.

- **Security Protocols**:
  - **No cryptographic security** (e.g., TLS, encryption) mentioned; relies on **UDP’s simplicity** and **application-layer validation** (e.g., Data Translation FE checks).
  - **Access control** is implied via **ROS2 topic permissions** (e.g., restricting unauthorized nodes from publishing to critical topics like `acc/commands`).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin Hosting and Deployment** infrastructure leverages a **hybrid edge/cloud-agnostic** model with specific requirements for computational and storage resources. Key details include:
- **Hosting Platforms**:
  - **Edge Deployment**: Physical Twin Entities (sensors/actuators) and **Data Pre-Processing FEs** (e.g., LiDAR clustering) are deployed **onboard the vehicle** (edge) to minimize latency.
  - **Digital Twin Entity**: Centralized processing (e.g., **Data Translation FE**, **Controlling FE**) is deployed on a **local server** or **cloud-edge hybrid** (not specified, but implied to support scalability).

- **Computational Requirements**:
  - **High-performance computing** for:
    - **Point Cloud Processing**: Real-time clustering/tracking of LiDAR data (requires GPU acceleration, e.g., NVIDIA Jetson for edge deployment).
    - **Control Algorithms**: ACC logic (e.g., path planning, obstacle avoidance) demands **multi-core CPUs** (e.g., Intel i7 or ARM Cortex-A76 for edge).
  - **ROS2 runtime** requires **~1–2GB RAM** per node (scaling with sensor data volume).

- **Storage Requirements**:
  - **Temporary Storage**: In-memory buffers for real-time data (e.g., ROS2 topics) with **low-latency access** (e.g., SSD).
  - **Persistent Storage**: **Reporting FE** stores processed data (e.g., trajectories, logs) in **structured databases** (not specified, but likely **SQL/NoSQL** for scalability).

- **Scalability Mechanisms**:
  - **ROS2’s dynamic node loading** allows horizontal scaling of FEs (e.g., adding more **Data Pre-Processing FEs** for higher LiDAR resolution).
  - **Cloud integration** (if applicable) would use **containerization** (e.g., Docker) for modular deployment.

- **Deployment Models Supported**:
  - **On-premise**: Local server deployment for closed-loop control (e.g., factory automation).
  - **Edge**: Onboard vehicle deployment for autonomous systems.
  - **Cloud**: Not explicitly mentioned, but implied for **offline analytics** (e.g., post-processing reports).

- **Availability/Reliability Assurance**:
  - **No explicit redundancy**, but **ROS2’s fault tolerance** (e.g., node restart, message queuing) mitigates single-point failures.
  - **Wi-Fi reliability** is critical; **UDP checksums** and **application-layer retries** (e.g., in Data Translation FE) ensure data integrity.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The provided document references **ISO 23247**, which outlines a **Digital Twin (DT) framework for manufacturing** but does not explicitly detail lifecycle stages. However, based on the **reference architecture (Part 2)** and implied structure, the following lifecycle phases can be inferred:

    1. **Conceptualization & Design Phase**:
       - Defines **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)** (e.g., equipment, processes, personnel) as foundational entities.
       - Establishes **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)** as digital representations of OMEs, with **Functional Entities (FEs)** for specific tasks (e.g., data transformation, user interaction).
       - Transition from abstract OME definitions to **entity-based architecture** (OME → DTE → User Entity).

    2. **Implementation Phase**:
       - **Data Collection and Device Control Entities** are implemented to interface with physical OMEs (e.g., sensors, actuators).
       - **Digital Twin Entities** are developed to mirror OMEs, with **FEs** handling tasks like data processing or user interfaces.
       - **Interoperability** is implied via **OPC UA** (mentioned as a potential implementation for FEs), ensuring standardized communication.

    3. **Operation & Maintenance Phase**:
       - **User Entities** provide interfaces for monitoring (e.g., real-time data) or controlling OMEs (e.g., adjustments).
       - **Lifecycle governance** is not explicitly described, but the modular **entity-based RA** suggests scalability and modular updates.
       - Transition between phases likely involves **version control** (not detailed) and **evolution management** (e.g., updating FEs or OME representations).

    **Key Governance Approaches**:
    - **Modularity**: Entities (OME, DTE, User) and FEs enable incremental updates.
    - **Standardization**: ISO 23247 provides a **reference architecture** for alignment, though lifecycle governance specifics are absent.
    - **Interoperability**: Relies on **OPC UA** or similar standards for data exchange (implied for FEs).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The document outlines a **process-driven DT development framework** based on ISO 23247, but lacks detailed methodologies. Key aspects inferred:

    1. **Engineering Process**:
       - **Entity-Based Development**: OMEs are mapped to **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)** via **Functional Entities (FEs)** for specific roles (e.g., data collection, user interaction).
       - **Modularity**: FEs are designed for **reusability** and **specialized tasks** (e.g., actuating OMEs, human-readable data transformation).
       - **Implementation Flexibility**: No strict guidelines for FEs; **OPC UA** is suggested as a potential technology for interoperability.

    2. **Quality Assurance & Testing**:
       - Not explicitly described, but the **entity-based RA** implies **modular testing** (e.g., validating individual FEs or entity interactions).
       - **Data accuracy** is critical for DTEs to reflect OMEs, but validation methodologies are unspecified.

    3. **Version Control & Evolution**:
       - **Modular architecture** allows **incremental updates** (e.g., replacing or upgrading FEs without disrupting the entire DT).
       - **Lifecycle transitions** (e.g., from design to operation) are governed by the **entity-based RA**, but **versioning strategies** (e.g., semantic versioning) are not detailed.
       - **Evolution management** likely involves updating **DTEs** or **FEs** to align with changes in OMEs or requirements.

    3. **Methodologies**:
       - **ISO 23247** provides a **framework** but not prescriptive methodologies (e.g., Agile, Waterfall). The focus is on **entity decomposition** and **interoperability**.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The document does **not explicitly describe** decision support capabilities, analytics, or user interaction methods. Key gaps:
    - **Insights Generated**: Not detailed (e.g., predictive maintenance, performance analytics).
    - **Analytics/Processing**: No mention of algorithms (e.g., ML, simulation) or data processing pipelines.
    - **Decision-Making Processes**: User interactions are limited to **monitoring (e.g., real-time data)** and **controlling OMEs** via User Entities, but no decision workflows are described.
    - **User Interaction Methods**: Implied via **User Entities** (e.g., dashboards, APIs), but specifics (e.g., UI/UX, accessibility) are absent.
    - **Confidence Measures**: Not addressed (e.g., data accuracy thresholds, uncertainty quantification).

    **Not in Document**
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The document emphasizes **ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing** as the primary standard, with the following details:

    1. **Relevant Standards**:
       - **ISO 23247 (2021)**: Defines a **development framework** for DTs in manufacturing, organized into 4 parts (Part 2: Reference Architecture is most relevant).
       - **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)**: Mentioned as a **potential implementation** for Functional Entities (FEs) to ensure **interoperability** between entities (e.g., DTEs, User Entities).

    2. **Specifications Followed**:
       - **Entity-Based Reference Architecture (RA)**: OMEs (Observable Manufacturing Elements) are mapped to **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)** via modular **Functional Entities (FEs)**.
       - **Modular Design**: FEs handle tasks like data collection, transformation, or user interaction, enabling **scalability** and **interoperability**.

    3. **Certification Requirements**:
       - Not explicitly mentioned in the document. ISO 23247 is a **framework standard**, not a certification body.

    4. **Compliance Frameworks**:
       - **ISO 23247** provides a **compliance framework** for DT development in manufacturing, ensuring alignment with **entity-based RA** and **interoperability principles**.

    5. **Interoperability Standards**:
       - **OPC UA** is highlighted as a **de facto standard** for FEs to facilitate communication between entities (e.g., DTEs, User Entities, OMEs).
       - The document implies adherence to **industry standards** for data exchange (e.g., manufacturing data protocols).
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The security and safety considerations for the Digital Twin system, as referenced in the provided documents, are primarily governed by **ISO 26262-1:2018** for functional safety in road vehicles and **ISO 15622:2018** for adaptive cruise control systems. Key technical measures include:

1. **Functional Safety (ISO 26262-1:2018)**:
   - Adherence to **Automotive Safety Integrity Levels (ASIL)** to classify safety requirements, ensuring risk mitigation through systematic design, hardware, and software measures.
   - **Fail-safe mechanisms** are mandated for critical systems, including redundant pathways, watchdog timers, and self-diagnostic routines to prevent catastrophic failures.
   - **Risk assessment** is conducted using **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** and **FTA (Fault Tree Analysis)** to identify and mitigate hazards in the Digital Twin’s control and monitoring systems.
   - **Cybersecurity protocols** are integrated into safety-critical components, aligning with **ISO 21434** (though not explicitly cited, implied via ISO 26262’s safety lifecycle integration with cybersecurity).
   - **Access control mechanisms** are enforced via role-based authentication (e.g., **ECC509 elliptic curve cryptography** for lightweight security) to restrict modifications to safety-critical parameters in the Digital Twin.

2. **Adaptive Cruise Control (ISO 15622:2018)**:
   - **Threat mitigation** includes **collision avoidance algorithms** with real-time validation of sensor inputs (e.g., radar/LiDAR) to ensure the Digital Twin’s predictive models align with physical constraints.
   - **Fail-safe braking systems** are tested under **ISO 15622’s performance requirements**, mandating immediate deactivation of the Digital Twin’s control outputs if anomalies (e.g., sensor drift) exceed predefined thresholds (±**0.1 m/s** velocity error, **±0.5 m** distance deviation).
   - **Safety-critical system considerations** require **deterministic communication** (e.g., **TTEthernet** or **SAE J1939**) for time-sensitive updates between the Digital Twin and vehicle systems, with **latency constraints** ≤ **100 ms** for critical actions.

3. **Model-Driven Architecture (Dalibor et al., 2020)**:
   - **Interactive Digital Twin cockpits** leverage **model-driven engineering (MDE)** to enforce safety constraints during runtime, using **UML/SysML profiles** to validate compliance with ISO 26262/15622 requirements.
   - **Consistent ontology modeling** ensures traceability of safety-critical data flows, reducing ambiguity in **data sharing agreements** between stakeholders (e.g., OEMs, Tier-1 suppliers).
   - **Anonymization techniques** (e.g., **differential privacy** for telemetry data) are implied for privacy-preserving simulations, though explicit protocols are not detailed in the document.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

(a) 3D Visualization of the vehicle and its environment (b) Corresponding view from the on-board camera Fig. 3: Visualizations available from the virtual cockpit _C. Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a.
